[0:04] No, Ukraine isn't fake.
[0:07] Hi, my name is Benoît. I make videos
[0:09] about history, especially the
[0:11] parts that people lie about.
[0:13] Today we're tackling
[0:14] something truly absurd.
[0:16] The idea that Ukraine isn't real, that
[0:18] it's a fake country, a Nazi
[0:20] invention, a Western puppet,
[0:23] all myths that are pushed by the Russian
[0:25] state to justify a brutal invasion.
[0:28] But history tells a different story. One,
[0:30] Russia is desperate to erase.
[0:33] Let's start with a basic fact.
[0:36] Ukraine is not a recent invention.
[0:39] The idea of Ukraine and of Ukrainians as
[0:41] a people is hundreds of years old.
[0:44] In medieval times, the powerful Kievan
[0:46] Rus was centered in what's now Kiev.
[0:49] Yeah, that Kiev, the one that Russia is
[0:52] bombing with missiles.
[0:54] Kievan Rus wasn't
[0:55] Russian in the modern sense.
[0:57] It was a multi-ethnic federation with
[1:00] deep Slavic roots and
[1:01] Orthodox Christianity.
[1:03] And it's claimed today by modern Russia,
[1:06] Belarus and also Ukraine.
[1:08] In reality, it was closer to Ukraine,
[1:11] geographically,
[1:12] culturally and politically,
[1:13] than anything we'd call Russia today.
[1:16] The Primary Chronicle, written around
[1:19] 1113, a foundational
[1:21] text of the Kievan Rus,
[1:23] places Kiev as the political and
[1:25] spiritual heart of the region.
[1:26] Not Novgorod or Moscow,
[1:29] which didn't even exist yet.
[1:31] Moscow didn't even appear in written
[1:33] records until the 1147 Chronicle,
[1:36] centuries after Kiev was
[1:37] already a major center.
[1:39] And when Moscow did emerge, it
[1:41] wasn't the heir to Kievan Rus,
[1:43] but one of several French principalities
[1:45] scrambling for power under Mongol rule.
[1:49] The myth of Moscow as the Third Rome, the
[1:52] rightful successor to
[1:53] Byzantium and the Rus,
[1:55] was political branding by the Moskowitz
[1:57] Church and State in the 15th century, not
[2:00] historical continuity.
[2:03] The letters of Philateus, written in the
[2:05] 16th century,
[2:06] proclaimed Moscow the Third Rome.
[2:09] This was ideological myth-making,
[2:12] designed to legitimize the Moskowitz rule
[2:14] after the fall of Constantinople, just as
[2:17] it was claimed by the Holy Roman Empire.
[2:20] For centuries after the fall of Kievan
[2:23] Rus, the lands of modern Ukraine were
[2:25] split between empires.
[2:27] Polish, Lithuanian,
[2:29] Ottoman, Habsburg and Russian.
[2:31] But Ukrainian identity survived, in
[2:34] songs, in language and religion,
[2:37] in the way people spoke about
[2:39] their land, not someone else's.
[2:41] In the 1600s, a semi-independent
[2:45] Cossack-Hetmanate
[2:47] rose in central Ukraine.
[2:49] Orthodox, militarized and fiercely
[2:52] autonomous, the Cossacks
[2:53] signed treaties with Polish kings
[2:56] and later aligned with the Moskowitz to
[2:58] protect their religious freedom.
[3:00] It was never a total submission.
[3:02] The 1654 Treaty of Perislav, often cited
[3:06] by Russian historians as
[3:07] proof of Ukraine joining Russia,
[3:10] was more like an alliance,
[3:13] conditional and contested.
[3:15] The articles of Perislav outlined
[3:17] specific Cossack demands for autonomy,
[3:20] many of which were
[3:20] later ignored by Moscow,
[3:23] sparking uprisings like the Helmenitsky
[3:26] Rebellion and the later Masepa Revolt.
[3:29] By the 18th century, the Hetmanate was
[3:31] dismantled and fully
[3:33] absorbed into the Russian Empire,
[3:35] but Ukrainian identity didn't vanish.
[3:38] Ukrainian intellectuals, often working in
[3:40] secret, kept the culture alive.
[3:43] That is, Sefchenko, born into Serfdom,
[3:46] became the voice of a people.
[3:49] His 1840s collection, Kopsar, was banned
[3:52] by Tsarist authorities, yet
[3:54] it circulated underground.
[3:56] His poems were not just
[3:57] literature, they were a declaration.
[4:00] We exist.
[4:02] Sefchenko's poem, "My Testament," became
[4:05] a national rallying cry.
[4:07] It was recited at funerals, protests and
[4:10] eventually independence rallies.
[4:13] When I am dead, bury me
[4:20] In a grave
[4:27] Among the wide steppe
[4:34] In my beloved Ukraine
[4:42] In 1917, the Russian Empire collapses and
[4:45] Ukraine declares independence.
[4:47] For a brief window, there was a real
[4:49] Ukrainian state, the
[4:51] Ukrainian People's Republic.
[4:53] It had a government, embassies and a
[4:55] military, even its own
[4:57] currency and postage stamps.
[5:00] The UPR issued its own banknotes and
[5:03] treaties, such as the 1918 Treaty of
[5:05] Brest-Litovsk, signed
[5:07] with central powers,
[5:08] and was recognized de
[5:09] facto by several states.
[5:12] The Republic was short-lived, it was
[5:14] crushed by the Bolsheviks
[5:15] during the Russian Civil War.
[5:17] The Red Army invaded Kiev multiple times
[5:19] between 1918 and 1921.
[5:22] Still, this idea of a
[5:24] sovereign Ukraine didn't vanish.
[5:26] Underground movements, cultural societies
[5:28] and partisan forces kept it alive even
[5:31] after the Republic fell.
[5:34] When Ukraine declared independence again
[5:36] in 1991, it wasn't inventing a country,
[5:39] it was restoring one.
[5:42] There is a myth pushed very hard by Putin
[5:45] that Ukraine only exists because of
[5:48] Lenin, that it was
[5:49] created by the Soviet Union.
[5:51] But that's not just
[5:52] wrong, it's propaganda.
[5:54] The truth is, Ukraine declared
[5:56] independence on its own, was crushed by
[5:58] force and then absorbed into a Soviet
[6:00] structure designed to control it.
[6:03] Lenin's 1919 letter, "The Question of
[6:05] Nationalities or Autonomization", reveals
[6:08] his concern about
[6:09] great Russian chauvinism.
[6:11] But his solution wasn't independence, it
[6:13] was tighter Soviet integration under the
[6:16] appearance of equality.
[6:19] The Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic
[6:21] was not freedom, it was a mask.
[6:24] Over the next decades, Ukrainian culture
[6:26] and autonomy were
[6:27] systematically dismantled.
[6:29] One of the most horrific moments came
[6:31] during the Holodomor, Stalin's man-made
[6:34] famine of 1932-1933, which killed an
[6:37] estimate of 3-4 million Ukrainians.
[6:41] US reports from 1933, now declassified,
[6:44] describe entire
[6:45] villages starving to death.
[6:48] Soviet archives confirm forced grain
[6:50] requisitions and the sealing of
[6:52] international borders to prevent escape.
[6:55] Even after the famine repression
[6:56] continued, Ukrainian language schools
[6:58] were closed, books were banned, artists
[7:01] and intellectuals were purged in the
[7:03] 1930s and again in the 1970s.
[7:07] Recification wasn't just
[7:08] policy, it was a war on identity.
[7:12] The 1972 arrest of poet Vasyl Sturs for
[7:15] anti-Soviet agitation is one of many, his
[7:18] crime, writing poems in Ukrainian and
[7:21] advocating for cultural freedom.
[7:24] So why does Russia
[7:26] say Ukraine isn't real?
[7:28] Because acknowledging Ukrainian statehood
[7:30] threatens the entire
[7:32] mythology of Russian imperialism.
[7:34] The idea that Russia is the natural
[7:36] center of all Slavs, that it's entitled
[7:38] to dominate its neighbors, that it must
[7:40] protect Russians
[7:41] everywhere, even if it means invasion.
[7:43] This isn't new, but it's been weaponized
[7:47] with renewed intensity since 2014, or
[7:50] some people would argue, 2008, when
[7:52] Russian troops went into Georgia.
[7:55] In his 2021 essay on the historical unity
[7:59] of Russians and Ukrainians, Vladimir
[8:01] Putin claimed that Russians and
[8:03] Ukrainians are one people, and that true
[8:06] Ukrainian sovereignty is only possible in
[8:09] partnership with Russia.
[8:10] He openly blames Lenin for ever allowing
[8:13] Ukraine to exist at all.
[8:16] This isn't about
[8:17] history, it's about control.
[8:19] In Putin's worldview, shaped by thinkers
[8:22] like Ivan Ilyin, a Russian immigrant
[8:24] fascist who's worked Putin frequently
[8:27] cites, Ukraine is an error that must be
[8:29] corrected, even by force.
[8:31] Ilyin's writings, revived in the 2000s by
[8:34] Putin's administration, stress national
[8:37] unity through spiritual authoritarianism.
[8:40] He viewed federalism and Ukrainian
[8:42] independence as
[8:43] threats to Russian greatness.
[8:46] Putin isn't reviving the USSR, he is
[8:49] reviving the Tsarist empire, one where
[8:52] Ukraine doesn't exist.
[8:54] But Ukraine does exist.
[8:57] It exists in blood, in culture, in
[8:59] memory, in centuries of resistance, from
[9:02] the Cossack Uprising to Sefshenko's
[9:05] poetry, to the defenders of Kiev today.
[9:07] It exists in the songs banned by the
[9:10] Tsars, in the grain fields where millions
[9:12] starved, but didn't break.
[9:14] In the voices of poets like Lina
[9:16] Kostenko, who wrote on her Soviet rule,
[9:19] "Nations do not die from gunfire, but
[9:21] from the loss of memory."
[9:22] And memory in Ukraine has always
[9:26] survived. No missile, no
[9:28] lie, no empire will erase that.
[9:32] Thanks for watching. If you found this
[9:34] valuable, consider
[9:35] liking, subscribing and sharing.
[9:38] This kind of history isn't always
[9:40] thought, but it's always worth knowing.
[9:44] Sources are linked in the description.
[9:46] Thank you very much for listening.
